objects.description: standard bed set low for walker transfers
nightstand on the far side of the bed, too low to brace against
loose area rug between bed and door
accent chair narrowing the route to the door
high wall shelf holding medications and a phone charger

rooms.notes: No visible light source in the hallway.

hazards.explanation: Loose rug on the walking path in the bedroom. A walker can catch on the edge and cause a fall.
Chair narrows the route in the bedroom to about 68cm, below the 90cm clearance needed for safe walker use.
Hallway lighting is dark. No visible light source is present on the hallway route. Nighttime trips to the bathroom are a major fall-risk scenario.
Bed height is borderline low at about 52cm. There is no stable support surface within reach for sit-to-stand transfers. Transfers are harder and less stable with a walker.
Essential items appear to be stored high on a shelf in the bedroom. Reaching overhead is unsafe during recovery.

recommendations.text: Remove the loose area rug between bed and door from the bedroom walking path. If it must stay, secure all edges flat with double-sided carpet tape.
recommendations.expected_benefit: Eliminates trip hazard on the bed-to-bathroom route

recommendations.text: Move the accent chair narrowing the route to the door to the far wall or corner of the bedroom to open at least 90cm of clear width for the walker.
recommendations.expected_benefit: Restores safe walker clearance on the bed-to-bathroom route

recommendations.text: Place a plug-in night light or motion-activated light in the hallway. The bathroom route must be visible without needing to find a switch at night.
recommendations.expected_benefit: Reduces nighttime misstep risk on the bathroom route

recommendations.text: The bed appears low for safe sit-to-stand transfers. Consider bed risers or discuss with an occupational therapist. A stable chair or grab handle beside the bed can also help.
recommendations.expected_benefit: Safer transfers, especially in early recovery days

recommendations.text: Move medications, chargers, and other daily essentials from the high shelf down to the nightstand within arm's reach of the bed. Nothing essential should require reaching above shoulder height.
recommendations.expected_benefit: Prevents unsafe reaching and reduces need to stand for basic items

first_night: Remove all loose rugs from the path between bed and bathroom.
Clear the walking path of any furniture, shoes, cords, or clutter.
Place the walker within arm's reach of the bed.
Put a night light in the hallway and bathroom.
Move water, phone, charger, and other daily essentials to the nightstand.
Make sure all doors on the bathroom route open fully and stay open.
Place a non-slip mat in the bathroom if the floor gets slippery.
Test that the patient can reach light controls before standing up.
first_48_hours: Install or arrange a grab bar near the toilet or shower area.
Practice the bed-to-bathroom route together during daytime.
Route all cords along walls instead of across walking paths.
Check whether bed height feels safe for sit-to-stand transfers.
Set up a bedside caddy with daily essentials in one place.
Confirm the patient can reach the phone or call device from bed at night.

disclaimer: This tool provides environmental suggestions to support recovery at home. It is not medical advice and does not replace guidance from your doctor, occupational therapist, or care team.
